title: Argument Game Proof Theories and Dialogues

questions.source_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
questions.text: An Argumentation Framework (AF) is formally defined as which of the following? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A pair $\langle \text{Args}, \text{Attacks} \rangle$ where Args is a set of arguments and Attacks $\subseteq$ Args $\times$ Args
A directed graph where nodes are arguments and edges represent attack relations
A triple $\langle \text{Args}, \text{Attacks}, \text{Supports} \rangle$ with both attack and support relations
A function from arguments to labels IN, OUT, or UN

explanations.text: This is the formal definition of an AF.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: An Argumentation Framework (AF) is a pair $\langle \text{Args}, \text{Attacks} \rangle$ where $\text{Args}$ is a set of arguments and $\text{Attacks} \subseteq \text{Args} \times \text{Args}$ is a binary attack relation.

explanations.text: An AF is equivalently described as a directed graph where nodes are arguments and directed edges represent attacks.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: An Argumentation Framework (AF) is a pair $\langle \text{Args}, \text{Attacks} \rangle$

explanations.text: The standard AF definition includes only attack relations, not support relations. Support is an extension found in bipolar AFs, not the basic definition.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings

explanations.text: A labelling is a function from arguments to labels — but that describes a labelling, not an AF itself.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings

questions.source_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
questions.text: Which conditions correctly define when a labelling assigns label IN to argument X? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every attacker of X is labelled OUT
X has no attackers at all
At least one attacker of X is labelled IN
X has no IN attackers and at least one UN attacker

explanations.text: The condition for IN is that every attacker is OUT.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathcal{L}(X) = \text{IN}$ iff every attacker of $X$ is labelled OUT

explanations.text: If X has no attackers, then vacuously every attacker is OUT, so X is labelled IN.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathcal{L}(X) = \text{IN}$ iff every attacker of $X$ is labelled OUT

explanations.text: Having at least one IN attacker is the condition for OUT, not IN.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathcal{L}(X) = \text{OUT}$ iff at least one attacker of $X$ is labelled IN

explanations.text: This is the condition for UN, not IN.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathcal{L}(X) = \text{UN}$ iff $X$ has no IN attackers and at least one UN attacker

questions.source_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the grounded labelling? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is the unique labelling with the smallest possible set of IN arguments
It is the most sceptical labelling
There may be multiple grounded labellings for a given AF
It has the largest possible set of IN arguments

explanations.text: The grounded labelling is unique and minimises the IN set.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling is the unique labelling with the smallest possible set of IN arguments (most sceptical).

explanations.text: Most sceptical means the fewest arguments are accepted as IN.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling is the unique labelling with the smallest possible set of IN arguments (most sceptical).

explanations.text: The grounded labelling is unique — there is exactly one grounded labelling per AF.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling is the unique labelling

explanations.text: Having the largest IN set describes preferred labellings, not the grounded labelling.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1: Recap: Argumentation Frameworks and Labellings
explanations.notes_quote: The preferred labelling(s) are labellings with the largest possible set of IN arguments (most credulous)

questions.text: What is the primary motivation for using argument game proof theories instead of computing labellings directly? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Computing labellings directly requires examining the whole framework, which may be expensive
Argument games allow checking whether a specific argument is IN without computing the full labelling
Argument games always produce results faster than direct computation for any AF
Direct labelling computation is impossible for frameworks with cycles

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state direct computation requires examining the whole framework.
explanations.notes_quote: Computing labellings directly requires examining the whole framework.

explanations.text: Argument games provide a targeted proof-theoretic approach for a specific argument.
explanations.notes_quote: Argument game proof theories provide an alternative: to check whether a specific argument $X$ is labelled IN, we play a two-player dialogue game rather than computing the full labelling.

explanations.text: The notes do not claim argument games are always faster — this is not stated and depends on the AF.
Direct labelling computation is possible for cyclic frameworks — cycles produce UN labels in grounded semantics.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the roles of PRO and OPP in an argument game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: PRO argues in favour of the argument under question and wants to show it wins
OPP argues against the argument and wants to show it loses
PRO always moves first
OPP always moves first
PRO and OPP move simultaneously on each turn

explanations.text: PRO is the proponent, arguing for the initial argument.
explanations.notes_quote: PRO (the Proponent): argues in favour of the argument under question. PRO wants to show the argument wins.

explanations.text: OPP is the opponent, arguing against.
explanations.notes_quote: OPP (the Opponent): argues against the argument. OPP wants to show it loses.

explanations.text: PRO always moves first — this is established in the basic mechanics.
explanations.notes_quote: The game is a turn-taking exchange of attack moves. PRO always moves first.

explanations.text: OPP does not move first — PRO always moves first.
explanations.notes_quote: PRO always moves first.

explanations.text: Moves are turn-taking, not simultaneous.
explanations.notes_quote: The game is a turn-taking exchange of attack moves.

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the game mechanics of an argument game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The initial move is PRO asserting the argument whose status is being questioned
Each subsequent move must be an argument that attacks the previous argument
A player loses a dispute if they cannot produce an attacking argument
PRO wins the overall game by winning at least one dispute
Backtracking allows a stuck player to try a different attack from a previous move

explanations.text: The initial move establishes the argument being argued for.
explanations.notes_quote: Initial move: PRO asserts an argument $X$ (the argument whose status is being questioned).

explanations.text: All moves must be attacks on the preceding move.
explanations.notes_quote: Subsequent moves: Each player responds to the previous move by producing an argument that attacks the previous argument.

explanations.text: Being stuck (no moves) means losing the dispute.
explanations.notes_quote: Losing condition: A player loses a dispute if they cannot produce an attacking argument (they are stuck with no moves).

explanations.text: PRO must win every dispute in the winning strategy, not just one. Winning a single dispute is insufficient.
explanations.notes_quote: PRO wins the overall game if PRO has a winning strategy — a set of disputes such that PRO wins every dispute in the strategy, regardless of which dispute OPP forces.

explanations.text: Backtracking lets a player try alternative attacks from an earlier position.
explanations.notes_quote: Backtracking: If a player is stuck in one branch, they may backtrack to a previous move and try a different attack, creating a new dispute.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly define argument game terminology? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A dispute is a path from the root to a leaf in the game tree
The game tree contains all possible moves from the initial move, with PRO and OPP nodes alternating
A winning strategy $T'$ is a subtree of the game tree such that PRO wins every dispute in $T'$
A dispute is the entire game tree including all branches
When asked to write a game tree, you only need to show one winning path

explanations.text: A dispute is one root-to-leaf path in the game tree.
explanations.notes_quote: Dispute: A path from the root to a leaf in the game tree

explanations.text: The game tree represents all possible plays.
explanations.notes_quote: Game tree: The full tree of all possible moves from the initial move, with PRO nodes and OPP nodes alternating

explanations.text: A winning strategy is a subtree in which PRO wins all disputes.
explanations.notes_quote: Winning strategy $T'$: A subtree of the game tree such that PRO wins every dispute in $T'$

explanations.text: A dispute is a single root-to-leaf path, not the entire game tree.
explanations.notes_quote: Dispute: A path from the root to a leaf in the game tree

explanations.text: You must write the complete game tree, not just one path. The notes explicitly say this.
explanations.notes_quote: When asked to write a game tree, you must write the complete game tree showing all possible moves, not just one path.

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the rules of the grounded argument game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Rule G1: Players alternate moves, with PRO moving first
Rule G2: Each move must be an argument that attacks the previous argument
Rule G3: PRO cannot repeat an argument already used in the same dispute
Rule G3: OPP cannot repeat an argument already used in the same dispute
The grounded game has four rules including a winning strategy condition

explanations.text: G1 establishes the turn-taking order.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule G1 (Turn-taking): Players alternate moves. PRO moves first.

explanations.text: G2 requires each move to attack the previous one.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule G2 (Attack): Each move must be an argument that attacks the previous argument.

explanations.text: G3 restricts PRO from repeating arguments, not OPP.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule G3 (PRO no-repeat): PRO cannot repeat an argument that PRO has already used in the same dispute.

explanations.text: In the grounded game, it is PRO who cannot repeat, not OPP. OPP cannot repeat only in the preferred game.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule G3 (PRO no-repeat): PRO cannot repeat an argument that PRO has already used in the same dispute.

explanations.text: The grounded game has three rules (G1, G2, G3). The extra winning strategy condition is only for the preferred game.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded game has three rules

questions.text: What is the rationale for Rule G3 (PRO no-repeat) in the grounded game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If PRO must repeat an argument, it indicates a cycle, and cyclic support should not count as IN in the grounded semantics
The grounded semantics is the most sceptical, so we are conservative about what is IN
PRO no-repeat prevents infinite games by ensuring termination
G3 corresponds to the preferred semantics wanting the largest consistent set

explanations.text: Cycles detected via PRO needing to repeat indicate the argument should not be IN.
explanations.notes_quote: If PRO must repeat an argument, it indicates a cycle — we do not want to count cyclic support as IN in the grounded semantics.

explanations.text: The grounded semantics is conservative (sceptical) about IN membership.
explanations.notes_quote: in the grounded labelling (the most sceptical semantics), we are conservative about what is IN.

explanations.text: While G3 may help termination, the stated rationale is about cyclic support and scepticism, not simply termination.
The largest consistent set rationale applies to preferred semantics and the WS condition, not G3 in the grounded game.

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the rules of the preferred argument game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Rule P1: Players alternate moves, with PRO moving first
Rule P3: OPP cannot repeat an argument already used in the same dispute
A winning strategy must satisfy: no two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other
Rule P3: PRO cannot repeat an argument already used in the same dispute
The preferred game has no additional conditions beyond P1, P2, P3

explanations.text: P1 is the same turn-taking rule as G1.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule P1 (Turn-taking): Players alternate moves. PRO moves first.

explanations.text: In the preferred game, OPP (not PRO) cannot repeat.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule P3 (OPP no-repeat): OPP cannot repeat an argument that OPP has already used in the same dispute.

explanations.text: The WS condition ensures the winning strategy corresponds to a conflict-free set.
explanations.notes_quote: Condition WS: No two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other.

explanations.text: In the preferred game, it is OPP not PRO who cannot repeat. PRO no-repeat is the grounded game rule G3.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule P3 (OPP no-repeat): OPP cannot repeat an argument that OPP has already used in the same dispute.

explanations.text: The preferred game has an additional Condition WS: no two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other.
explanations.notes_quote: Condition WS: No two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other.

questions.text: What is the rationale for Condition WS (no two PRO arguments attacking each other) in the preferred game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It ensures PRO's arguments form a conflict-free set, matching what preferred labellings require
The preferred semantics seeks the largest consistent set of IN arguments, and consistency means no self-attack
It prevents PRO from using the same argument twice in one dispute
It corresponds to the grounded semantics being the most sceptical

explanations.text: The WS condition enforces conflict-freeness for PRO's arguments across the whole strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: The no-mutual-attack condition on PRO's arguments ensures the winning strategy corresponds to a consistent (conflict-free) set — exactly what preferred labellings require.

explanations.text: Preferred labellings maximise IN while remaining conflict-free.
explanations.notes_quote: in the preferred labelling, we want the largest consistent set of IN arguments.

explanations.text: Preventing PRO from repeating in a dispute is Rule G3 (grounded game), not Condition WS.
Condition WS is specific to the preferred game; the grounded semantics is captured by G3 (PRO no-repeat).

questions.text: Which comparisons between the grounded and preferred games are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the grounded game, PRO cannot repeat in a dispute; in the preferred game, OPP cannot repeat
The grounded game establishes membership in the grounded labelling; the preferred game establishes membership in some preferred labelling
The preferred game has an additional winning-strategy condition absent from the grounded game
Both games restrict both PRO and OPP from repeating arguments
The grounded game has an extra winning-strategy condition; the preferred game does not

explanations.text: The key distinction: PRO no-repeat for grounded, OPP no-repeat for preferred.
explanations.notes_quote: PRO cannot repeat in dispute: Yes (G3) [Grounded]. OPP cannot repeat in dispute: Yes (P3) [Preferred].

explanations.text: Each game's winning strategy characterises a different semantics.
explanations.notes_quote: Semantics established: Grounded labelling [Grounded]. Preferred labelling [Preferred].

explanations.text: The WS condition (no two PRO args attacking each other) is only in the preferred game.
explanations.notes_quote: Extra condition on winning strategy: None [Grounded]. No two PRO args in $T'$ attack each other [Preferred].

explanations.text: Each game restricts only one player from repeating: PRO in grounded, OPP in preferred.
It is the preferred game that has the extra winning-strategy condition, not the grounded game.
explanations.notes_quote: Extra condition on winning strategy: None [Grounded]. No two PRO args in $T'$ attack each other [Preferred].

questions.text: Which conditions must a subtree $T'$ satisfy to be a winning strategy for PRO? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The root of $T'$ is PRO's initial argument
Every OPP node in $T'$ has all its children included
Every PRO node in $T'$ has at least one child included
Every leaf of $T'$ is a node at which OPP is stuck
Every PRO node in $T'$ has all its children included

explanations.text: The winning strategy must be rooted at the initial PRO argument.
explanations.notes_quote: The root of $T'$ is PRO's initial argument.

explanations.text: OPP can play any available move, so all OPP choices must be covered.
explanations.notes_quote: Every OPP node in $T'$ has all its children included (OPP can play any move).

explanations.text: PRO only needs to choose one response (not all) at each PRO node.
explanations.notes_quote: Every PRO node in $T'$ has at least one child included (PRO chooses one response).

explanations.text: Every leaf must be a position where OPP cannot move — meaning PRO wins that dispute.
explanations.notes_quote: Every leaf of $T'$ is a node at which OPP is stuck (OPP cannot move).

explanations.text: PRO nodes only need at least one child in $T'$, not all children. PRO chooses one response; it is OPP nodes that need all children included.
explanations.notes_quote: Every PRO node in $T'$ has at least one child included (PRO chooses one response).

questions.source_ref: Section 4.2: How to Identify a Winning Strategy
questions.text: What is the correct procedure for identifying a winning strategy from a game tree? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Write the complete game tree for the game
Identify all disputes (root-to-leaf paths)
Find a subtree where all OPP nodes have all children included and all PRO nodes have at least one child, with all leaves won by PRO
For the preferred game, additionally verify no two PRO arguments in the subtree attack each other
Select only the disputes where PRO wins and declare any single winning dispute as the winning strategy

explanations.text: The complete game tree must be written first.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: How to Identify a Winning Strategy
explanations.notes_quote: Write the complete game tree for the game.

explanations.text: All disputes must be identified to check which PRO wins.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: How to Identify a Winning Strategy
explanations.notes_quote: Identify all disputes (root-to-leaf paths).

explanations.text: This is the structural definition of a winning strategy subtree.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: How to Identify a Winning Strategy
explanations.notes_quote: Find a subtree where every OPP node's children are all included and every PRO node has at least one child, such that all leaves are won by PRO.

explanations.text: The WS condition must be verified for the preferred game.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: How to Identify a Winning Strategy
explanations.notes_quote: For preferred game: additionally verify no two PRO arguments in the subtree attack each other.

explanations.text: A single winning dispute is insufficient. A winning strategy must cover all OPP moves. OPP nodes must have all children in the strategy.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: How to Identify a Winning Strategy

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
questions.text: Consider AF: $A \to B$, $B \to A$. Which statements about the grounded game for 'Is A IN?' are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: PRO plays A; OPP plays B; PRO must attack B but cannot repeat A (Rule G3) and has no other attacker; PRO is stuck
PRO has no winning strategy in the grounded game for A
A is NOT in the grounded labelling, consistent with A being labelled UN
PRO wins the grounded game because it can repeat A to counter B
Both A and B are UN in the grounded labelling of this AF

explanations.text: PRO cannot repeat A due to G3 and has no other attacker of B, so gets stuck.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: PRO must attack B and cannot repeat A (Rule G3). PRO has no other attacker of B, so PRO is stuck.

explanations.text: There is no winning strategy for PRO in the grounded game for A.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: PRO has no winning strategy → A is not in the grounded labelling.

explanations.text: A is not IN in the grounded labelling; it is UN.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: Consistent with A = UN.

explanations.text: In the grounded game, PRO cannot repeat (G3). Repeating A is allowed only in the preferred game.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: PRO must attack B and cannot repeat A (Rule G3).

explanations.text: The mutual attack creates a symmetric cycle where both are UN in the grounded labelling.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: Grounded labelling: Both $A$ and $B$ are UN (odd even cycle — UN/UN is the grounded labelling).

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
questions.text: For the same AF ($A \to B$, $B \to A$), which statements about the preferred game for 'Is A IN?' are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: PRO plays A; OPP plays B; PRO plays A again (allowed, since P3 restricts OPP not PRO); OPP cannot repeat B (P3) and is stuck
PRO has a winning strategy in the preferred game for A
A is in a preferred labelling of this AF
The WS condition is satisfied because PRO only played A (no two distinct PRO args attack each other)
The preferred labellings of this AF are $\{A=\text{IN}, B=\text{OUT}\}$ and $\{A=\text{OUT}, B=\text{IN}\}$

explanations.text: In the preferred game OPP cannot repeat, so after playing B once OPP is stuck when PRO plays A again.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: PRO plays A (attacks B). OPP cannot repeat B (Rule P3) and has no other attacker of A, so OPP is stuck.

explanations.text: PRO wins the dispute and has a winning strategy.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: PRO has a winning strategy → A is in a preferred labelling.

explanations.text: A is IN in the preferred labelling {A=IN, B=OUT}.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: Consistent with A = IN in the preferred labelling $\{A=\text{IN}, B=\text{OUT}\}$.

explanations.text: PRO only used A, so there are no two distinct PRO arguments to attack each other.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: Check no-mutual-attack: PRO only played A once, so no two PRO args attack each other.

explanations.text: The two preferred labellings correspond to each argument being IN while the other is OUT.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: Simple Example A attacks B, B attacks A
explanations.notes_quote: Preferred labellings: $\{A = \text{IN}, B = \text{OUT}\}$ and $\{A = \text{OUT}, B = \text{IN}\}$.

questions.text: In the running example ($D \to C \to B \to A$, $E \to B$, $F \to A$), which statements about the grounded game for 'Is A IN?' are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There are three disputes: D1 (A→F→stuck), D2 (A→B→E→stuck), D3 (A→B→C→D→stuck)
PRO wins dispute D2 because E has no attackers and OPP is stuck
PRO loses dispute D1 because F has no attackers so PRO is stuck after OPP plays F
PRO has a winning strategy because it wins D2
A is NOT in the grounded labelling because D1 cannot be won

explanations.text: The three disputes are as described, branching on OPP's first move (F or B) and PRO's second move (E or C).
explanations.notes_quote: Three disputes total: D1: A → F → PRO stuck. D2: A → B → E → OPP stuck. D3: A → B → C → D → PRO stuck.

explanations.text: In D2, E has no attackers so OPP is stuck after PRO plays E.
explanations.notes_quote: D2: A → B → E → OPP stuck. PRO wins.

explanations.text: When OPP plays F, PRO cannot attack F (no attackers of F), so PRO is stuck.
explanations.notes_quote: D1: A → F → PRO stuck. PRO loses.

explanations.text: Winning D2 alone is not enough. A winning strategy must cover all OPP moves. OPP can also play F (leading to D1 which PRO loses). So no winning strategy exists.
explanations.notes_quote: For a winning strategy PRO would need to win all disputes in the strategy, including D1. But D1 cannot be won. No winning strategy exists.

explanations.text: Since D1 cannot be won and OPP can force D1, there is no winning strategy for PRO.
explanations.notes_quote: No winning strategy exists → A is not in the grounded labelling. Consistent with A = OUT.

questions.text: Regarding the preferred vs grounded game comparison in Section 5.3, which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the preferred game, PRO can repeat an argument; this allows PRO to win when OPP is restricted by P3
In the grounded game, PRO gets stuck when forced to cycle due to Rule G3
An argument can be in a preferred labelling but not in the grounded labelling
In the preferred game, OPP can repeat an argument since P3 only restricts PRO

explanations.text: PRO has no repeat restriction in the preferred game, enabling it to cycle and force OPP into a stuck position.
explanations.notes_quote: Preferred game — PRO can win by repeating A. In a preferred game, PRO is allowed to repeat. When OPP uses an argument and PRO can counter it with A again (because P3 restricts OPP not PRO), the game can terminate in PRO's favour.

explanations.text: G3 prevents PRO from repeating, so PRO gets stuck when a cycle is encountered in the grounded game.
explanations.notes_quote: Grounded game — Same AF: PRO cannot repeat (G3). PRO gets stuck when forced to cycle.

explanations.text: This is the key result — an argument may be in a preferred labelling but not the grounded labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: PRO has a winning strategy → argument is in a preferred labelling. PRO has no winning strategy → argument is not in the grounded labelling.

explanations.text: P3 restricts OPP from repeating, not PRO. OPP cannot repeat in the preferred game.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule P3 (OPP no-repeat): OPP cannot repeat an argument that OPP has already used in the same dispute.

questions.text: For an AF with an odd cycle ($A \to B \to C \to A$), which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the grounded game, PRO gets stuck due to the no-repeat rule G3 and cannot win
Arguments in odd cycles end up as UN in the grounded labelling
The grounded game correctly reflects that odd-cycle arguments are not IN in the grounded labelling
PRO can win the grounded game in an odd cycle by using backtracking

explanations.text: The no-repeat rule G3 prevents PRO from cycling, leading to PRO being stuck.
explanations.notes_quote: In the grounded game, the game will cycle indefinitely (or PRO gets stuck due to no-repeat rule G3) and PRO cannot win.

explanations.text: Odd-cycle arguments are labelled UN in the grounded labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: arguments in odd cycles are not in the grounded labelling (they end up UN).

explanations.text: The game result (PRO cannot win) correctly corresponds to the labelling (UN, not IN).
explanations.notes_quote: This is correct: arguments in odd cycles are not in the grounded labelling (they end up UN).

explanations.text: Backtracking does not help in an odd cycle — PRO still gets stuck because G3 prevents any repeat and all paths cycle.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.5: LGT Sheet Example Triangle AF
questions.text: For the triangle AF ($A \to B$, $B \to C$, $C \to A$), which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: All three arguments are UN in the grounded labelling
The preferred game tree for 'Is A IN?' has 12 disputes
The winning strategy for the preferred game on A consists of disputes d3 and d9
Efficiency Rule 1 states PRO cannot move X if X attacks or is attacked by an argument Y already moved by PRO in the same dispute
A is IN in the grounded labelling of the triangle AF

explanations.text: In a pure 3-cycle, all arguments end up UN in the grounded labelling.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: LGT Sheet Example Triangle AF
explanations.notes_quote: For the triangle, all three arguments end up UN in the grounded labelling.

explanations.text: The full preferred game tree for this AF has 12 disputes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: LGT Sheet Example Triangle AF
explanations.notes_quote: The full game tree from the LGT answers has 12 disputes d1–d12.

explanations.text: The winning strategy identified in the LGT answers uses disputes d3 and d9.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: LGT Sheet Example Triangle AF
explanations.notes_quote: The winning strategy consists of disputes d3 and d9.

explanations.text: This is Efficiency Rule 1 from the LGT answers.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: LGT Sheet Example Triangle AF
explanations.notes_quote: Efficiency Rule 1: PRO cannot move argument $X$ if $X$ attacks or is attacked by an argument $Y$ already moved by PRO in the same dispute.

explanations.text: A is UN (not IN) in the grounded labelling due to the cyclic structure.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: LGT Sheet Example Triangle AF
explanations.notes_quote: For the triangle, all three arguments end up UN in the grounded labelling.

questions.text: In SGT AF1 ($B \to C$, $D \to C$, $G \to D$, $H \to G$, $E \to C$), for the grounded game 'Is H IN?', which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The grounded labelling gives H = IN, G = OUT, D = IN, C = OUT, B = IN, E = OUT
The single winning dispute is: PRO H → OPP G → PRO D → OPP C → PRO B, with OPP stuck at B
H is in the grounded labelling
The grounded game for H requires multiple disputes to establish a winning strategy
B has no attackers, so when PRO plays B, OPP is stuck and PRO wins

explanations.text: This is the grounded labelling from the solutions.
explanations.notes_quote: Grounded labelling of AF1: $B = \text{IN}$, $C = \text{OUT}$, $D = \text{IN}$, $E = \text{OUT}$, $G = \text{OUT}$, $H = \text{IN}$

explanations.text: The single dispute follows the chain H→G→D→C→B, ending with OPP stuck.
explanations.notes_quote: Single dispute: $H \to G \to D \to C \to B$, OPP stuck at B. PRO wins.

explanations.text: H is in the grounded labelling as confirmed by the winning strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: H is in the grounded labelling.

explanations.text: The grounded game for H has a single dispute forming the winning strategy; multiple disputes are not needed.
explanations.notes_quote: Winning strategy: this single dispute.

explanations.text: B has no attackers, so OPP cannot respond when PRO plays B.
explanations.notes_quote: PRO: B ← OPP has no move (B has no attackers) → PRO wins

questions.text: Regarding SGT AF2, which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The grounded labelling has G = IN, H = OUT, K = IN
Arguments A through F are all UN in the grounded labelling of AF2
F is in at least one preferred labelling of AF2
Two winning strategies exist for the preferred game 'Is F IN?'
F is IN in the grounded labelling of AF2

explanations.text: The isolated chain G→H→K gives G=IN, H=OUT, K=IN.
explanations.notes_quote: $G = \text{IN}$, $H = \text{OUT}$, $K = \text{IN}$

explanations.text: Arguments A–F are all UN in the grounded labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: $A = \text{UN}$, $B = \text{UN}$, $C = \text{UN}$, $D = \text{UN}$, $E = \text{UN}$, $F = \text{UN}$

explanations.text: Two winning strategies for the preferred game confirm F is in at least one preferred labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: Two winning strategies exist (from solutions). Each dispute in the winning strategy is itself a separate winning strategy — indicating $F$ is in at least one preferred labelling.

explanations.text: The solutions identify two winning strategies for the preferred game on F.
explanations.notes_quote: Two winning strategies exist (from solutions).

explanations.text: F is UN in the grounded labelling, not IN.
explanations.notes_quote: $F = \text{UN}$

questions.text: Which conditions define a grounded dispute formally? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The sequence alternates between PRO moves (even indices) and OPP moves (odd indices)
For each $i > 0$: $X_i$ attacks $X_{i-1}$
No PRO argument appears twice in the sequence
No OPP argument appears twice in the sequence
$X_0$ is an argument chosen by OPP

explanations.text: G1 requires alternation with PRO at even indices.
explanations.notes_quote: G1: The sequence alternates between PRO moves (even indices) and OPP moves (odd indices).

explanations.text: G2 requires each move to attack the preceding one.
explanations.notes_quote: G2: For each $i > 0$: $X_i$ attacks $X_{i-1}$ (i.e., $(X_i, X_{i-1}) \in \text{Attacks}$).

explanations.text: G3 (PRO no-repeat) is the no-repetition condition for PRO.
explanations.notes_quote: G3: No PRO argument appears twice in the sequence (PRO no-repeat).

explanations.text: OPP no-repeat is Rule P3 in the preferred game, not a condition of grounded disputes.
$X_0$ is PRO's initial argument, not OPP's. PRO always moves first.

questions.text: The Theorem connecting grounded games to labellings states: (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $X_0$ is in the grounded labelling iff PRO has a winning strategy in the grounded game for $X_0$
PRO has a winning strategy iff every OPP node in the subtree has all its children in $T'$, every PRO node has at least one child, and every leaf is a stuck OPP position
$X_0$ is in the grounded labelling iff PRO wins at least one dispute in the grounded game
$X_0$ is in a preferred labelling iff PRO has a winning strategy in the grounded game

explanations.text: This is the soundness and completeness theorem for the grounded game.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem: $X_0$ is in the grounded labelling iff PRO has a winning strategy in the grounded game for $X_0$.

explanations.text: These are the structural conditions defining a winning strategy.
explanations.notes_quote: Every OPP node has all its children in $T'$. Every PRO node has at least one child in $T'$. Every leaf of $T'$ is a node where OPP has no move.

explanations.text: Winning one dispute is insufficient. A winning strategy must cover all OPP moves.
The grounded game characterises the grounded labelling, not preferred labellings. Preferred labellings are characterised by the preferred game.

questions.text: Which conditions define a preferred winning strategy $T'$ formally? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: No OPP argument appears twice in the dispute sequence (P3)
No two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other (Condition WS)
$X_0$ is in some preferred labelling iff PRO has a winning strategy in the preferred game for $X_0$
No two OPP arguments in $T'$ attack each other
PRO nodes in $T'$ must have all their children included

explanations.text: P3 is the OPP no-repeat rule for preferred disputes.
explanations.notes_quote: P3: No OPP argument appears twice in the sequence (OPP no-repeat).

explanations.text: Condition WS is the additional requirement on preferred winning strategies.
explanations.notes_quote: WS: No two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other.

explanations.text: This is the theorem connecting the preferred game to preferred labellings.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem: $X_0$ is in some preferred labelling iff PRO has a winning strategy in the preferred game for $X_0$.

explanations.text: The no-mutual-attack condition applies to PRO's arguments, not OPP's arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: WS: No two PRO arguments in $T'$ attack each other.

explanations.text: PRO nodes only need at least one child in $T'$, not all children. It is OPP nodes that need all children in $T'$.
explanations.notes_quote: Every PRO node has at least one child in $T'$.

questions.text: How do dialogues differ from argument games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Argument games are played between two agents with shared knowledge; dialogues generalise to multiple agents with distributed knowledge
In dialogues, each agent may know only part of the relevant information
Dialogues require a central knowledge repository to function
Dialogues formalise communication through locutions and protocols

explanations.text: Dialogues extend games to multi-agent distributed settings.
explanations.notes_quote: An argument game is played between two agents (PRO and OPP) with shared knowledge. A dialogue generalises this to multiple agents with distributed knowledge — each agent knows only part of the relevant information.

explanations.text: Distributed knowledge means each agent has partial information.
explanations.notes_quote: each agent knows only part of the relevant information.

explanations.text: Dialogues enable distributed reasoning without a central knowledge repository.
explanations.notes_quote: agents reason jointly without needing a central knowledge repository.

explanations.text: Locutions and protocols formalise the communication in dialogues.

questions.text: Which of the following are correctly defined locutions in dialogue theory? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{claim}(q)$: Agent asserts claim $q$
$\text{why}(q)$: Agent challenges claim $q$, asking for justification
$\text{argue}(X, q)$: Agent presents argument $X$ with conclusion $q$
$\text{retract}(q)$: Agent withdraws a previously made claim $q$

explanations.text: claim(q) is one of the three defined locution types.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{claim}(q)$: Agent asserts claim $q$

explanations.text: why(q) is a challenge locution.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{why}(q)$: Agent challenges claim $q$, asking for justification

explanations.text: argue(X,q) is the argument-presenting locution.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{argue}(X, q)$: Agent presents argument $X$ with conclusion $q$ to support or rebut

explanations.text: retract is not one of the three locution types defined in the notes. The three types are claim, why, and argue.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the Soundness and Completeness results (SC1, SC2, Combined SC) for dialogues? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: SC1: A claim $p$ is winning in the locution graph iff $p$ is the conclusion of a justified argument in the AF implicitly defined by the locution graph
SC2: Argument $X$ is a winning argument in the AF iff the conclusion $q$ of $X$ is non-monotonically inferred from the knowledge base
Combined SC: A dialogue establishes claim$(q)$ iff $q$ is a non-monotonic inference from all exchanged knowledge
Combined SC: A dialogue establishes claim$(q)$ iff $q$ is classically provable from all exchanged knowledge

explanations.text: SC1 links locution graph winning claims to justified arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: SC1: A claim $p$ is winning in the locution graph iff $p$ is the conclusion of a justified argument in the AF implicitly defined by the locution graph.

explanations.text: SC2 connects winning arguments in the AF to non-monotonic inference.
explanations.notes_quote: SC2: Argument $X$ is a winning argument in the AF iff the conclusion $q$ of $X$ is non-monotonically inferred from the knowledge base.

explanations.text: The combined SC result is the core theoretical result linking dialogues to non-monotonic reasoning.
explanations.notes_quote: Combined SC: A dialogue establishes $\text{claim}(q)$ iff $q$ is a non-monotonic inference from all the knowledge exchanged during the dialogue.

explanations.text: The Combined SC concerns non-monotonic inference, not classical provability. Classical and non-monotonic inference are different.
explanations.notes_quote: Combined SC: A dialogue establishes $\text{claim}(q)$ iff $q$ is a non-monotonic inference from all the knowledge exchanged during the dialogue.

questions.text: Which statements correctly characterise distributed non-monotonic reasoning via dialogues? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Multiple agents with partial knowledge can engage in a structured dialogue to jointly derive non-monotonic inferences
The conclusion of the dialogue equals the non-monotonic inference from pooling all agents' knowledge bases
The dialogue protocol ensures only relevant knowledge is exchanged
Agents require a central knowledge repository to achieve non-monotonic inference in dialogues

explanations.text: Dialogues enable joint non-monotonic reasoning with partial knowledge.
explanations.notes_quote: Multiple agents, each with partial knowledge, can engage in a structured dialogue.

explanations.text: The dialogue result equals what would be inferred by pooling all knowledge.
explanations.notes_quote: The conclusion of the dialogue (whatever claim is established) is exactly the non-monotonic inference that would follow from pooling all the agents' knowledge bases together.

explanations.text: The protocol controls what knowledge gets exchanged.
explanations.notes_quote: The dialogue protocol ensures that only relevant knowledge is exchanged.

explanations.text: Dialogues explicitly enable reasoning without a central repository.
explanations.notes_quote: agents reason jointly without needing a central knowledge repository.

sections.title: Applications and Value Alignment (LO4 — Not Examined)

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the value alignment problem? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is the challenge of ensuring AI systems make decisions aligned with human values and preferences
Human values are complex, context-dependent, and often conflicting
Different people or cultures may have different values, making alignment non-trivial
The value alignment problem has been fully solved by modern LLMs

explanations.text: This is the definition of the value alignment problem.
explanations.notes_quote: The value alignment problem is the challenge of ensuring that AI systems make decisions that are aligned with human values and preferences.

explanations.text: The complexity of human values is a core challenge.
explanations.notes_quote: Human values are complex, context-dependent, and often conflicting.

explanations.text: Cultural and individual variation adds difficulty.
explanations.notes_quote: Different people or cultures may have different values.

explanations.text: The notes describe alignment as a critical ongoing challenge — not as solved.
explanations.notes_quote: As AI systems become more capable and autonomous, ensuring alignment is increasingly critical.

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe SocrAPPs? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: SocrAPPs are LLM-based tools designed to conduct Socratic dialogues
Their goal is to inculcate critical thinking rather than simply providing answers
The LLM takes the role of a Socratic interlocutor, asking probing questions
SocrAPPs operate without any connection to argumentation theory
The locution graph in a SocrAPP dialogue implicitly defines an AF

explanations.text: SocrAPPs conduct Socratic dialogues.
explanations.notes_quote: SocrAPPs (Socratic Applications) are LLM-based tools designed to conduct Socratic dialogues

explanations.text: The goal is critical thinking development.
explanations.notes_quote: The goal is to inculcate critical thinking in users rather than simply providing answers.

explanations.text: The LLM acts as the Socratic questioner.
explanations.notes_quote: The LLM takes the role of a Socratic interlocutor, asking probing questions.

explanations.text: SocrAPPs are explicitly connected to argumentation theory — the locution graph defines an AF and the SC results apply.
explanations.notes_quote: SocrAPPs instantiate the dialogue theory formalised in LO3

explanations.text: The LLM-user exchange creates a locution graph implicitly defining an AF.
explanations.notes_quote: The locution graph implicitly defines an AF.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state key distinctions between grounded and preferred semantics for argument games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the grounded game PRO cannot repeat; in the preferred game OPP cannot repeat
A winning strategy is a subtree, not a single dispute — it must cover all OPP choices
The preferred extra condition (WS) requires no two PRO arguments in the winning strategy to attack each other
For the exam procedure, you should write only the winning path rather than the complete game tree
The grounded labelling is unique; preferred labellings may be multiple

explanations.text: This is the core distinction: PRO restricted in grounded, OPP restricted in preferred.
explanations.notes_quote: In the grounded game, PRO cannot repeat. In the preferred game, OPP cannot repeat.

explanations.text: A winning strategy must cover all OPP possibilities.
explanations.notes_quote: A winning strategy is a subtree covering all OPP choices, not just one path. PRO must be able to respond to every possible OPP move.

explanations.text: This is Condition WS, the additional constraint on preferred winning strategies.
explanations.notes_quote: Even if PRO wins every dispute in a subtree, the subtree is only a valid preferred winning strategy if no two PRO arguments in it attack each other.

explanations.text: The exam procedure requires writing the complete game tree first, not just one path.
explanations.notes_quote: Exam procedure: Always write the complete game tree first, then identify the winning strategy (or lack thereof) as a subtree.

explanations.text: Uniqueness of grounded labelling and multiplicity of preferred labellings are key properties.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling is the unique labelling with the smallest possible set of IN arguments. The preferred labelling(s) are labellings with the largest possible set of IN arguments; there may be more than one.
